packs.name: AWS CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive AWS CLI operations like terminate-instances, delete-db-instance, and s3 rm --recursive

destructive_patterns.name: ec2-terminate
destructive_patterns.reason: aws ec2 terminate-instances permanently destroys EC2 instances.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
terminate-instances permanently destroys EC2 instances:

- Instance is stopped and deleted
- Instance store volumes are lost
- EBS root volumes deleted (unless DeleteOnTermination=false)
- Elastic IPs are disassociated

This cannot be undone. The instance ID will never be reusable.

Preview first:
  aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids i-xxx

Consider stop instead:
  aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids i-xxx

destructive_patterns.name: removes AWS resources
destructive_patterns.reason: aws ec2 delete-* permanently removes AWS resources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
EC2 delete commands permanently remove resources:

- delete-snapshot: Removes EBS snapshot (backup data lost)
- delete-volume: Destroys EBS volume and all data
- delete-vpc: Removes VPC (must be empty)
- delete-image: Deregisters AMI
- delete-security-group: Removes firewall rules
- delete-key-pair: Removes SSH key (can't SSH to instances using it)

Always verify resource IDs:
  aws ec2 describe-<resource> --<resource>-ids xxx

destructive_patterns.name: s3-rm-recursive
destructive_patterns.reason: aws s3 rm --recursive permanently deletes all objects in the path.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
s3 rm --recursive deletes ALL objects under the specified path:

- All files and 'folders' are deleted
- Versioned objects: only current version deleted
- No trash/recycle bin
- Cannot be undone (unless versioning enabled)

Preview what would be deleted:
  aws s3 ls s3://bucket/path/ --recursive
  aws s3 rm s3://bucket/path/ --recursive --dryrun

Consider versioning for recovery:
  aws s3api list-object-versions --bucket bucket

destructive_patterns.name: s3-sync-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws s3 sync --delete removes files from the destination not present in the source.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
s3 sync --delete synchronizes the destination with the source and DELETES files from the destination that don't exist in the source:

- Files unique to the destination are permanently deleted
- Wrong source/dest order can wipe out the destination
- No trash or undo mechanism

Preview what would change:
  aws s3 sync s3://source/ s3://dest/ --delete --dryrun

Consider syncing without --delete:
  aws s3 sync s3://source/ s3://dest/

suggestions.description: Preview what would be synced and deleted without making changes
Sync without --delete: only adds/updates, never removes

destructive_patterns.name: s3-rb
destructive_patterns.reason: aws s3 rb removes the entire S3 bucket.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
s3 rb removes an S3 bucket:

- Bucket must be empty (use --force to delete contents first)
- With --force: deletes all objects then bucket
- Bucket name becomes available for others
- Cannot be undone

Check bucket contents:
  aws s3 ls s3://bucket --recursive --summarize

Verify bucket name:
  aws s3api head-bucket --bucket bucket-name

destructive_patterns.name: s3api-delete-bucket
destructive_patterns.reason: aws s3api delete-bucket removes the entire S3 bucket.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
s3api delete-bucket removes a bucket (must be empty):

- Returns error if bucket not empty
- Bucket name released for reuse by anyone
- Associated policies and configurations lost

Empty bucket first if needed:
  aws s3 rm s3://bucket --recursive

Or use s3 rb --force for both operations.

destructive_patterns.name: rds-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws rds delete-db-instance/cluster permanently destroys the database.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
RDS delete commands permanently remove database resources:

- delete-db-instance: Destroys the database instance
- delete-db-cluster: Destroys Aurora cluster
- delete-db-snapshot: Removes backup
- delete-db-cluster-snapshot: Removes cluster backup

Consider:
- Create final snapshot before deletion
- Skip final snapshot only for test instances

Create backup:
  aws rds create-db-snapshot --db-instance-id xxx --db-snapshot-id backup

destructive_patterns.name: cfn-delete-stack
destructive_patterns.reason: aws cloudformation delete-stack removes the entire stack and its resources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
CloudFormation delete-stack removes the stack AND all resources it created:

- EC2 instances terminated
- RDS databases deleted (unless DeletionPolicy: Retain)
- S3 buckets removed (if empty)
- All IAM resources deleted

Resources with DeletionPolicy: Retain are kept but orphaned.

Preview resources:
  aws cloudformation describe-stack-resources --stack-name xxx

Consider:
  aws cloudformation delete-stack --retain-resources res1 res2

destructive_patterns.name: lambda-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws lambda delete-function permanently removes the Lambda function.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-function removes a Lambda function completely:

- Function code is deleted
- All versions and aliases removed
- Event source mappings deleted
- Cannot be undone

Backup function code first:
  aws lambda get-function --function-name xxx --query Code.Location

List versions:
  aws lambda list-versions-by-function --function-name xxx

destructive_patterns.name: iam-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws iam delete-* removes IAM resources. Verify dependencies first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
IAM delete commands remove identity resources:

- delete-user: Removes IAM user (must detach policies first)
- delete-role: Removes role (must detach policies first)
- delete-policy: Removes managed policy
- delete-group: Removes IAM group

Check dependencies:
  aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name xxx
  aws iam list-entities-for-policy --policy-arn xxx

Roles used by services (Lambda, EC2) will break!

destructive_patterns.name: dynamodb-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws dynamodb delete-table permanently deletes the table and all data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-table removes a DynamoDB table and ALL its data:

- All items are deleted
- Table configuration is lost
- Global secondary indexes deleted
- Cannot be undone

Backup first:
  aws dynamodb create-backup --table-name xxx --backup-name backup

Or export to S3:
  aws dynamodb export-table-to-point-in-time ...

destructive_patterns.name: eks-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: aws eks delete-cluster removes the entire EKS cluster.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-cluster removes an EKS cluster:

- Control plane is deleted
- Node groups must be deleted separately first
- Kubernetes resources (deployments, services) are lost
- Persistent volumes may remain as orphaned EBS

Delete node groups first:
  aws eks list-nodegroups --cluster-name xxx
  aws eks delete-nodegroup --cluster-name xxx --nodegroup-name yyy

Then delete cluster.

destructive_patterns.name: ecr-delete-repository
destructive_patterns.reason: aws ecr delete-repository permanently deletes the repository and its images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-repository removes an ECR repository:

- All images in the repository are deleted
- Repository configuration lost
- Requires --force if repository not empty

List images first:
  aws ecr list-images --repository-name xxx

Consider keeping critical images:
  docker pull <account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com/repo:tag

destructive_patterns.name: ecr-batch-delete-image
destructive_patterns.reason: aws ecr batch-delete-image permanently deletes one or more images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
batch-delete-image removes specific images from ECR:

- Images are permanently deleted
- Can delete by tag or digest
- Running containers using these images may fail on restart

List images:
  aws ecr describe-images --repository-name xxx

Verify image usage before deletion.

destructive_patterns.name: ecr-delete-lifecycle-policy
destructive_patterns.reason: aws ecr delete-lifecycle-policy removes the repository lifecycle policy.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-lifecycle-policy removes automatic image cleanup rules:

- Old images will no longer be automatically deleted
- May lead to storage cost increases
- Repository will retain all images indefinitely

View current policy:
  aws ecr get-lifecycle-policy --repository-name xxx

destructive_patterns.name: logs-delete-log-group
destructive_patterns.reason: aws logs delete-log-group permanently deletes a log group and all events.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-log-group removes a CloudWatch log group:

- All log streams are deleted
- All log events are lost
- Metric filters and subscriptions removed
- Cannot be undone

Export logs before deletion:
  aws logs create-export-task --log-group-name xxx \
    --destination bucket --from 0 --to $(date +%s)000

destructive_patterns.name: logs-delete-log-stream
destructive_patterns.reason: aws logs delete-log-stream permanently deletes a log stream and all events.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
delete-log-stream removes a specific log stream:

- All events in the stream are deleted
- Log group remains intact
- Cannot be undone

View log stream events before deletion:
  aws logs get-log-events --log-group-name xxx \
    --log-stream-name yyy --limit 100

packs.name: Google Cloud SDK
packs.description: Protects against destructive gcloud operations like instances delete, sql instances delete, and gsutil rm -r

destructive_patterns.name: compute-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud compute instances delete permanently destroys VM instances.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
compute instances delete permanently destroys VMs:

- Instance is stopped and deleted
- Boot disk deleted (unless --keep-disks specified)
- External IPs released back to pool
- Instance metadata and logs lost

Use --keep-disks=boot,data to preserve disks for recovery.

destructive_patterns.name: disk-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud compute disks delete permanently destroys disk data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
compute disks delete permanently destroys persistent disks:

- All data on the disk is lost forever
- Cannot be recovered without snapshots
- Any instances using the disk will fail

Create a snapshot before deletion: gcloud compute disks snapshot DISK

destructive_patterns.name: sql-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud sql instances delete permanently destroys the Cloud SQL instance.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
sql instances delete permanently destroys Cloud SQL:

- Database and all data deleted
- All backups deleted (unless retained)
- Read replicas also deleted
- IP addresses released

Export data first: gcloud sql export sql INSTANCE gs://bucket/file.sql

destructive_patterns.name: gsutil-rm-recursive
destructive_patterns.reason: gsutil rm -r permanently deletes all objects in the path.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
gsutil rm -r recursively deletes all objects:

- All objects under the path are deleted
- Cannot be recovered without versioning enabled
- -m flag parallelizes (faster but same risk)

List first: gsutil ls -r gs://bucket/path/
Enable versioning: gsutil versioning set on gs://bucket

destructive_patterns.name: gsutil-rb
destructive_patterns.reason: gsutil rb removes the entire GCS bucket.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
gsutil rb removes the entire Cloud Storage bucket:

- Bucket must be empty (or use -f to force)
- Bucket name becomes available to others
- All bucket-level permissions lost

List contents first: gsutil ls gs://bucket/

destructive_patterns.name: gke-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud container clusters delete removes the entire GKE cluster.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
container clusters delete removes the entire GKE cluster:

- All nodes and workloads terminated
- Persistent volumes may be deleted
- Load balancers and IPs released
- Cluster-level secrets lost

Backup workloads: kubectl get all -A -o yaml > backup.yaml

destructive_patterns.name: project-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud projects delete removes the entire GCP project and ALL its resources!
destructive_patterns.explanation:
projects delete removes the ENTIRE GCP project:

- ALL resources in the project deleted
- All VMs, databases, storage, functions
- All IAM policies and service accounts
- 30-day recovery window, then permanent

This is the most destructive GCP command possible!

destructive_patterns.name: functions-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud functions delete removes the Cloud Function.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
functions delete removes Cloud Functions:

- Function code and configuration deleted
- Triggers and event subscriptions removed
- Function URL becomes unavailable

Export source first if not in version control.

destructive_patterns.name: pubsub-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud pubsub delete removes Pub/Sub topics or subscriptions.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
pubsub delete removes messaging infrastructure:

- Topic deletion removes all subscriptions
- Unacknowledged messages are lost
- Publishers will fail until recreated

Check subscribers: gcloud pubsub topics list-subscriptions TOPIC

destructive_patterns.name: firestore-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud firestore delete removes Firestore data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
firestore delete removes Firestore documents:

- Documents and collections deleted
- Subcollections may remain (delete recursively)
- No automatic backups by default

Export first: gcloud firestore export gs://bucket/backup

destructive_patterns.name: container-images-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud container images delete permanently deletes container images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
container images delete removes images from GCR:

- Image tags and digests deleted
- Running containers unaffected (cached)
- New pulls will fail

List tags first: gcloud container images list-tags IMAGE

destructive_patterns.name: artifacts-docker-images-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud artifacts docker images delete permanently deletes container images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
artifacts docker images delete removes images from Artifact Registry:

- Specified image version deleted
- Other tags pointing to same digest unaffected
- Consider cleanup policies instead

List versions: gcloud artifacts docker images list REPO

destructive_patterns.name: artifacts-repositories-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: gcloud artifacts repositories delete permanently deletes the repository.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
artifacts repositories delete removes entire repository:

- All packages/images in repository deleted
- Repository configuration lost
- IAM policies on repository removed

List contents: gcloud artifacts packages list --repository=REPO

packs.name: Azure CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive Azure CLI operations like vm delete, storage account delete, and resource group delete

destructive_patterns.name: vm-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az vm delete permanently destroys virtual machines.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
vm delete permanently destroys Azure VMs:

- VM is deallocated and deleted
- OS disk deleted (unless --os-disk=detach)
- Data disks detached but not deleted
- Public IP released

Preserve disks: az vm delete --os-disk detach --data-disks detach

destructive_patterns.name: storage-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az storage account delete permanently destroys the storage account and all data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
storage account delete destroys entire storage account:

- ALL blobs, files, queues, tables deleted
- All containers and their contents gone
- Cannot be recovered without backups

List contents first: az storage container list --account-name NAME

destructive_patterns.name: blob-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az storage blob/container delete permanently removes data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
storage blob/container delete removes data:

- Blob delete removes individual blobs
- Container delete removes container and ALL blobs
- Soft delete may allow recovery if enabled

Check soft delete: az storage account show --name NAME --query blobServiceProperties

destructive_patterns.name: sql-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az sql server/db delete permanently destroys the database.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
sql server/db delete destroys databases:

- Server delete removes ALL databases on server
- Database delete removes specific database
- Point-in-time restore possible within retention period

Create backup: az sql db export --name DB --server SRV --storage-uri URI

destructive_patterns.name: group-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az group delete removes the entire resource group and ALL resources within it!
destructive_patterns.explanation:
group delete removes ENTIRE resource group:

- ALL resources in the group deleted
- VMs, storage, databases, networks - everything
- Cannot be undone
- --no-wait returns immediately (deletion continues)

This is one of the most destructive Azure commands!

destructive_patterns.name: aks-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az aks delete removes the entire AKS cluster.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
aks delete removes the entire Kubernetes cluster:

- All nodes and workloads terminated
- Persistent volumes may be deleted
- Load balancers and IPs released
- Node resource group also deleted

Backup workloads: kubectl get all -A -o yaml > backup.yaml

destructive_patterns.name: webapp-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az webapp delete removes the App Service.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
webapp delete removes App Service:

- Application code and configuration deleted
- Custom domain mappings removed
- SSL certificates may be deleted
- Deployment slots also deleted

Backup config: az webapp config show --name NAME -g RG

destructive_patterns.name: functionapp-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az functionapp delete removes the Azure Function App.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
functionapp delete removes Azure Functions:

- All functions and configuration deleted
- Triggers and bindings removed
- Function keys lost
- Associated storage may be affected

Export functions if not in version control.

destructive_patterns.name: cosmosdb-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az cosmosdb delete permanently destroys the Cosmos DB resource.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
cosmosdb delete destroys Cosmos DB resources:

- Account delete removes entire Cosmos DB account
- Database delete removes database and all containers
- Collection delete removes container and data

Enable point-in-time restore for recovery options.

destructive_patterns.name: keyvault-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az keyvault delete removes the Key Vault. Secrets may be unrecoverable.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
keyvault delete removes Key Vault:

- All secrets, keys, certificates deleted
- Soft delete allows recovery (if enabled)
- Purge protection prevents permanent deletion
- Vault name reserved for recovery period

Check protection: az keyvault show --name NAME --query properties.enablePurgeProtection

destructive_patterns.name: vnet-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az network vnet delete removes the virtual network.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
network vnet delete removes virtual network:

- Network must be empty (no subnets in use)
- Connected resources lose connectivity
- Peerings to other VNets broken
- Network security groups may remain

Check usage: az network vnet subnet list --vnet-name VNET -g RG

destructive_patterns.name: acr-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az acr delete removes the container registry and all images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
acr delete removes entire container registry:

- ALL repositories and images deleted
- All tags and manifests gone
- Webhooks and replications removed
- Registry name becomes available to others

List repos: az acr repository list --name REGISTRY

destructive_patterns.name: acr-repository-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: az acr repository delete permanently deletes the repository and its images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
acr repository delete removes repository:

- All tags and images in repository deleted
- Running containers unaffected (cached)
- New pulls will fail

List tags: az acr repository show-tags --name REG --repository REPO

destructive_patterns.name: acr-repository-untag
destructive_patterns.reason: az acr repository untag removes tags from images.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
acr repository untag removes image tags:

- Tag removed from manifest
- Image still exists if other tags reference it
- Untagged images may be garbage collected

Lower risk: manifests can be re-tagged if digest known.

packs.name: DigitalOcean
packs.description: Protects against destructive DigitalOcean operations like droplet deletion

destructive_patterns.name: droplet-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: droplet delete permanently removes the droplet and its data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a droplet permanently destroys the virtual machine and its local data:

- All data on the droplet is lost
- The IP address may be released
- Cannot be undone unless a snapshot or backup exists

Verify the droplet ID or name before deleting:
  doctl compute droplet list

Take a snapshot first:
  doctl compute droplet-action snapshot <droplet_id>

packs.name: Heroku
packs.description: Protects against destructive Heroku operations like apps:destroy

destructive_patterns.name: apps-destroy
destructive_patterns.reason: apps:destroy permanently deletes the app and all its resources.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Destroying a Heroku app removes the application and all its configuration:

- All add-ons are removed (and their data may be deleted)
- All environment variables (Config Vars) are lost
- The app name is released
- Cannot be undone

Verify the app name before destroying:
  heroku apps:info --app <app_name>

Check add-ons first:
  heroku addons --app <app_name>

packs.name: Vercel
packs.description: Protects against destructive Vercel operations like vercel rm

destructive_patterns.name: vercel-rm
destructive_patterns.reason: vercel rm permanently removes a deployment or project.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Removing a Vercel project or deployment is destructive:

- All associated resources and data may be deleted
- The project name or deployment URL will no longer be available
- Operation is irreversible

Verify what you are removing first:
  vercel ls

Inspect a specific deployment:
  vercel inspect <deployment_url>

packs.name: Netlify
packs.description: Protects against destructive Netlify operations like sites:delete

destructive_patterns.name: sites-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: sites:delete permanently deletes a Netlify site.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Netlify site removes the site and all its deployments:

- All associated resources and site settings are lost
- The site ID is released
- Cannot be undone

Verify the site ID or name before deleting:
  netlify sites:list

Check site details:
  netlify sites:get --site <site_id>
